exemplar.title: Kinetic Friction between a soccer ball and different moistures of grass

detailedExemplarData.title: Kinetic Friction between a soccer ball and different moistures of grass

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: All data processing is carried out accurately and consistently, with correct application of the energy-balance equation
Clear and precise communication of raw and processed data in well-labelled tables and graphs with error bars, trend line, Pearson r and p-value
Appropriate uncertainty analysis including propagation for displacement averages, gradient and calculated uâ values
Demonstrated understanding of outlier testing and treatment (IQR method)
Detailed derivation of the core equation linking measured quantities to the coefficient of friction
weaknesses: The assumption of rolling without slipping and neglect of air resistance could be stated more explicitly in the uncertainty analysis
Justification for the Â±4% uncertainty on grass moisture is not given
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand of the data-analysis criterion is in the top band: processing is accurate, communication is both clear and precise, and the student gives appropriate consideration to uncertainties. Minor idealization omissions do not materially detract from an otherwise exemplary analysis, justifying the maximum score of 6.

strengths: Clear, specific research question framed in a concrete soccer-pitch context
Comprehensive identification and justification of manipulated, dependent, controlled and uncontrolled variables
Detailed tables (Table 1 and Table 2) demonstrating careful planning to limit confounding factors
Use of a ramp to standardize initial velocity and six trials for sufficient trend analysis
weaknesses: Some controls (grass length, ambient temperature) are not addressed
The method for verifying grass moisture in mm is not specified
Omissions of exact ramp angle/length and calibration of measuring equipment introduce minor ambiguities
Inconsistency between stated number of trials (five) and data tables (six)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student situates the research question within a specific, real-world context and provides a largely reproducible procedure. Methodological considerations are described in sufficient detail to allow replication with few ambiguities, but some experimental controls and calibration steps remain unspecified. Overall, performance fits solidly within the 5â6 band, meriting a score of 5.

strengths: Conclusion clearly states that uâ decreases with increasing grass moisture and links directly to the strong negative correlation (r â â0.962, p = 0.027)
Justification is fully consistent with the data analysis and refers back to the original hypothesis
Acknowledges minor caveats (e.g., risks in linear extrapolation) demonstrating critical awareness
weaknesses: Comparison to accepted scientific context (tire friction on wet pavement) is relevant but brief
No quantitative reconciliation between published values and the studentâs results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student delivers a conclusion that is justified and fully consistent with the analysis, earning a top-band score for relevance and support. The comparison to broader scientific context is pertinent but not deeply developed, placing the second strand in the 3â4 band and leading to an overall score of 5.

strengths: Identifies and explains the impact of specific methodological weaknesses (uneven grass causing bounce, wet ramp/ball) on measured uâ values
Proposes realistic, relevant improvements (e.g., mowing the field, drying equipment between trials, increasing moisture resolution) linked directly to those weaknesses
Demonstrates understanding of how each improvement would quantitatively reduce error sources and improve data quality
weaknesses: Quantitative estimates of how each suggested change would alter uncertainties are not provided
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands of the evaluation criterion fall within the top band: specific weaknesses are clearly explained, and realistic, relevant improvements are not only described but also justified in terms of their effect on experimental accuracy. The minor absence of numerical impact estimates does not prevent awarding the maximum score of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states a clear research question that specifies both variables (grass moisture quantified in mm of simulated rain and the kinetic coefficient of friction) and indicates how the coefficient will be obtained.  A short introductory paragraph links the problem directly to real-world play on wet soccer pitches, identifying why the question matters to players.  Because the context is concrete (soccer performance under different rainfall conditions) and explicitly connected to the investigation, the description meets the rubric requirement for a âspecific and appropriate context.â  Some minor phrasing issues (e.g., spelling of âOuestionâ) do not detract from the contextualization, but the discussion is relatively brief and could have integrated quantitative background (e.g., typical rainfall on pitches) to be outstanding.  Hence the strand is best placed in the 5-6 band, with a mark of 5.

Uncertainties are propagated for displacement averages, moment of inertia, watering rate, gradient and, to some extent, the calculated u_k values.  Error bars are included on the graph and maximum/minimum gradients used to estimate gradient uncertainty.  While full propagation through equation 14 is not carried out rigorously for every point, the studentâs treatment shows appropriate consideration of both random (range/2) and systematic (apparatus) sources.  Because remaining omissions (e.g., combined % uncertainty on u_k) are relatively minor, the strand fits within âappropriate consideration,â earning a 5.

The student identifies manipulated, dependent, controlled and uncontrolled variables in well-structured tables and justifies each.  Five moisture levels and six trials provide a data set that is sufficient to reveal a trend.  Methodological considerations (e.g., keeping initial velocity constant with a ramp, using same ball/field, accounting for watering-rate uncertainty) are described but not deeply analysed; some controls such as grass length or ambient temperature are not addressed, and the justification for leaving air resistance uncontrolled is qualitative only.  Consequently the work satisfies âdescribedâ rather than âexplained,â fitting the 3-4 band; a score of 4 reflects that most relevant considerations are covered competently.

Two specific methodological weaknesses are identified (uneven grass causing bounce; increasing wetness of ramp/ball), and for each the student explains qualitatively how the error biases u_k (e.g., larger than actual) or affects initial velocity.  Impact is discussed in terms of energy and displacement, demonstrating understanding of relative influence.  The explanation could be strengthened with quantitative estimates, so a score of 5 (rather than 6) is appropriate within the top band.

The procedure lists numbered steps, apparatus, measurement ranges and uncertainties, and an image/diagram of the set-up.  From the text another researcher could in principle reproduce the work: ramp height (0.18 m), ball mass/radius, moisture levels and watering rate (0.25 mm sâ»Â¹), number of trials, displacement measurement with a trundle wheel, and the equation used are all provided.  However, some operational details are missing or ambiguousâfor example the exact ramp angle/length, precise area to be watered, whether the grass is mowed, and the method for verifying 0 mm moisture.  These omissions would force a replicating student to make assumptions.  Therefore the description is âreproducible with few ambiguities,â matching the 3-4 band; the fuller end of that band (4) is awarded because the majority of necessary information is present.

The student correctly applies equation 14 using measured quantities, converts displacement to metres, rounds sensibly and includes an uncertainty for each u_k.  Outlier testing via IQR is demonstrated.  Calculations shown match tabulated results, and plotted values align with the table.  Although the physics model assumes rolling without slipping and neglects air resistance, those assumptions are stated; numerical work is internally consistent and accurate.  Hence processing is âcarried out appropriately and accurately,â placing it in the top band with a score of 5 (small physics idealisation issues preclude a perfect 6).

Improvements such as mowing the field, drying the ramp/ball between trials, and increasing the moisture resolution are realistic and clearly linked to the identified weaknesses.  The student explains how each change would reduce specific errors (e.g., minimise bouncing to isolate frictional losses), indicating an understanding of experimental design refinement.  Because the rationale is clear but not quantitatively justified, the strand secures a 5 in the 5-6 band.

Raw and processed data are presented in clearly labelled tables with units and absolute uncertainties.  The graph includes axes labels, units and error bars; trend line, Pearson r and p-value are shown.  Some formatting glitches (split tables over pages) reduce polish slightly, yet overall the data communication is both clear (easily readable) and precise (correct units, dp consistency, uncertainty notation).  This meets the 5-6 band; a mark of 5 is assigned to acknowledge minor presentational lapses that stop it short of flawless.

The student compares findings to a published study on tire friction on wet versus dry pavement, noting the similarity (negative correlation) but also the difference in materials.  This constitutes ârelevant comparison,â but it is brief and does not deeply justify or quantitatively reconcile differences (e.g., magnitude of u_k).  Consequently the strand aligns with the 3-4 band, at the higher end (4) because the comparison is pertinent and explicitly discussed.

The conclusion explicitly states that u_k decreases with increasing grass moisture, cites the strong negative correlation (r â â0.962) and p = 0.027, and refers back to the hypothesis.  The argument consistently links processed data, gradient sign and uncertainty analysis, satisfying the requirement that the conclusion be âjustified and fully consistent with the analysis.â  Minor caveats about linear extrapolation show sound reasoning but are not extensively quantified; therefore the strand merits a solid 5 within the 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: The materials list includes many entries (e.g., multiple apps, cameras) that are tangential to core procedure. Streamlining to essential apparatus would improve clarity.
Apparatus errors for Logger Pro and the friction app are specified preciselyâthis level of detail supports rigorous uncertainty analysis.
The introduction provides a clear purpose but could be strengthened by including quantitative context (e.g., typical rainfall on soccer pitches) to justify the chosen moisture levels more robustly.
The research question explicitly states both variables and measurement method, situating the investigation in a concrete soccer scenarioâthis meets the criteria for a well-contextualized question.
The background definition of kinetic friction is accurate but generic. Tie the description directly to grass-ball interactions (e.g., surface roughness of grass) to deepen relevance.
The theoretical derivation of energy relations is thorough but could refer explicitly to rolling without slipping to clarify the physical assumptions.
Figure 1 and its caption are informative, but the placement splits the text awkwardly. Consider repositioning or resizing to improve readability.

The background covers translational vs. rotational energy but omits how uncertainties in velocity and radius propagate into the friction calculationâaddressing this would strengthen methodological transparency.
The derivation of Equation 14 is clear and correctly links energy conservation to measured displacementâthis provides a solid foundation for data processing.
The derivation does not explicitly note the assumption of rolling without slipping. Stating such assumptions makes the modelâs applicability more transparent.
Numbering of equations skips from (14) to later references without consistency. Ensure every equation is numbered in sequence to aid readability.
Table 1 clearly identifies manipulated and measured variables with apparatus and uncertaintiesâthis is a strong presentation of variable selection.
It is unclear how grass moisture in mm was measured or verified. Specify the method (e.g., depth probe, volumetric water content) to improve reproducibility.
Consider including a systematic calibration of the trundle wheel readings (e.g., checking against a taped distance) to verify the apparatus error estimate.
Table 2 lists controlled variables comprehensively, including wind and ball selectionâthis demonstrates careful planning to limit confounding factors.
The strategy for wind control relies on subjective observation rather than quantitative measurement. Consider using an anemometer to ensure wind speed remains below a threshold.
Implementing the ramp to standardize initial velocity is a strong control measure, improving consistency across trials.
The procedure states five trials, but the data tables show six. Ensure consistency between method description and data collection to avoid confusion.
The procedure does not explain how the desired mm of moisture is quantified or verified. Adding a moisture measurement protocol (e.g., probe readings) would enhance reproducibility.
The risk assessment identifies key hazards and mitigations comprehensively, demonstrating awareness of safety and environmental considerations.
Procedure step 2â3 omits instruction on calibrating the trundle wheel. Include a calibration step to verify distance measurements against a known standard.
Including qualitative observations about ball bouncing enriches understanding of surface variability and supports discussion of systematic errors.
The qualitative data note bouncing but do not link directly to its impact on calculated friction values. Consider quantifying bounce height or frequency for clearer error analysis.
The raw displacement table includes six trials and average Â± apparatus error, with clear labeling and consistent significant figuresâexcellent communication of data processing.
The Â±4% uncertainty on grass moisture is listed at the table heading but not justified in the methodology. Explain how this percentage was derived.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
